speaker: interviewee
text: So as we were entrained-- we all mounted into the compartments-- was a regular train. And we went, and we went, and we went via northern France. I knew the route because in the black marketeering, I had traveled it with trucks, underneath of the trucks, smuggling money, smuggling anything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Excuse me. And so the sites of Lilllers, Saint-Omer, that was all familiar. Finally we arrived on the 27th, in the morning, about 5:30, in Boulogne-sur-Mer, which is a port. Boulogne-sur-Mer is situated right kitty corner from Dover. It's Calais, Boulogne-sur-Mer, and Dover, the English coast. In the summertime you can see the coast of England. We all get out of the train. We jump into two trucks. We're there. And we're being hauled into a camp. From above the ridge we could see the camp site. One huge place with sand and gravel-- small. Three barracks and that was it. You step out the trucks and we stand in line with our suitcases next to us, whatever we had. And there's a blond SS man and a dark haired SS man coming toward us. They wore the police uniform. They were inspectors who had a tiny little black SS here. They were already integrated into the SS. The black-haired one looks at us and he says in this perfect military German, this looks like a pigsty. You can't even stand at attention when an officer comes. We need to teach you. We will do a little gymnastics. Early in the morning it's good for you. Gymnastics for the Germans is like boot camp in America. It's called sport. You do two-hour sport. Which is OK for a little guy like me. I was an athlete. It's not so OK for 60 year olds who had never done anything like this in their lifetime. By the time the two hours were over, about six of them died of a heart attack right there. The other ones were bloodied, torn clothing. We looked like hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are dismissed to clean up. And when I go to the barrack, which we were shown to go to, the blond-haired SS man with a former inmate, and I knew the gentleman. He was the oldest of the camp. I knew him from Berlin and he knew me when I was a boy. His name was Schleisinger, Alvin Schleisinger. The blond-haired SS man stops me. And I'm a typical German. I stand at attention. He says, uh huh. He looks at me and says, [GERMAN]? Are you sure that you're a Jew? I said, [GERMAN]. He says, your mother a Jew? I said, yes. Your father a Jew? Yes. Put your pants down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every German in the military, the SS, had a firm belief that a boy who was circumcised was a Jew. I don't know where they got the history from, because the whole Arab nations are circumcised. Every Arab is circumcised. But, then, that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: He saw it. Put up the pants again. Hmpf. He looks at Irvin and says, Alvin, you know the boy. He says, yeah. You sure he's a Jew? He says, yeah. Well. In typical German he says, I will never believe it. I think they sent him from Berlin to spy on me that I do a good job. Can you believe such nonsense? Such idiocy? And, you know, I was always treated well in the camp by him. Never had problem from him. He always-- whenever he saw me would talk to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was afraid of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, as we are in the camp, other people come into the camp. One week passes, we go to work in the morning. First of all, when we went into the barrack I got assigned on a cot on the bottom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And out of Antwerp was a young fellow with the name of David, who I knew for my scout days in '39. And he slept next me. We became very dear friends, fortunately. Another transport came the next day with another friend of mine with the name Jackie Benkofske. If he's still alive, he's in Brussels. He came back. And so we were the trio so to speak. We got up at five o'clock in the-- before that I needed to go to the bathroom. This is my initiation, the sport, and the initiation to go to the bathroom. I went to Alvin and I said, Alvin, I've got to go. He says, go in the corner. I went to the corner. There was an open big pit with planks across, nine feet deep, and that was it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't believe my eyes. That was an initiation. No medical facilities. No sanitary facilities. No hygienic facilities. The water was miserable. You could only drink boiled water just like in Auschwitz later on. It was cold. You had no soap. No inmate clothing. You had your own clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next morning we go to work. Five o'clock in the morning up. Excuse me, what was the name of this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Had no name. Nobody has ever written or talked about it but me. It's in no book. I'm the only one who wrote about it in a book, in mine. And just Boulogne-sur-Mer. Arbeitslager. Working camp. And by six o'clock we formed the commandos, or the unit of work. And we march out for about half an hour. And we arrive on the coast. We were building bunkers or fortifications against the English coast. And what you did is you had cigarettes, or you rolled cigarettes in those days. You had one hand on the shovel and one hand you rolled a cigarette. And then you smoked it. When he came then you would fast work. This is-- You worked with your eyes. You learned that very easily and very fast. You don't work with your physical strength because you ain't got none. You've got about a quart of soup a day, a pint of coffee or whatever it was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] excuse me. And about 300 grams of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the cigarettes?

speaker: interviewee
text: You merchandised. You sliced a slice of bread and you exchanged it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where would people have gotten--

speaker: interviewee
text: From outside. Asking the meister, the foreman, and whatever have you. Because you did work with civilians. And so now you work. What you did is, besides shoveling the earth, or whatever it is, you carried cement sacks from the train to the workplace, where they would mix the cement with gravel and make concrete. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And so that was all right if it was dry. On the third day when we did that, it started drizzling and raining. This is a coast. And this is wrapped in paper. So the paper of course splits. The cement goes on you. And you're filthy, you're wet, it soaks in. You go back to the camp and instead of cleaning yourself because you're so dog tired, you're worn out, you're hungry, you go to bed the way you are, on the straw mattress. The next morning you get soaking wet up again with the same clothing. And you go back to work. What this basically creates is lice, filth. After about the fourth day I had so many lice I could make a record in clipping them, and killing them. This is what we did all night long. Unfortunately, after about two weeks, a week and a half, I got scabs on my legs and on my arms. Because it was dirty, it was filthy. I was one who got scabs. One thing the Germans are deadly afraid of is contagious diseases. They have an abhorrence for this. So the blond SS mans looks at my scabs. He says, ah, [GERMAN] In the hospital. So Alvin looks at me and says, go to the hospital. I come into the hospital. We were three fellows. One broken a arm, his arm, another one a finger, and then me with the scabs. I said goodbye to David and Jackie. Went into the hospital. Was l'hopital St. Pierre.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was manned by Catholic nuns, Franciscan nuns. They were nurses. So I went to the-- they sent me first of all, I came into the front office and the gal there said, vous parlez francais? Do you speak French? I says, oui. I says, oh, god. We have some of your people here that don't speak any French. We don't know what to do with them. And Dr. Bellou came by and says, do you speak French? I says, yes. He says, how come? So I told him that I went to school in Brussels.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, ah, that's terrific. You can be the translator. I'll keep you here for as long as I can. You won't have to go back. So don't worry about. I says, hey, the sun is coming. Man, this is terrific. And they send me to the pavilion, the contagious pavilion. And I will never forget the sister, the nun, Soeur St. Benoit. Probably the gentlest person I've ever met in my lifetime. She gave me one great, big look at my arms, because I didn't take my pants off. This is a nun there. I didn't know what a nun was, just out of books.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said, well, we're going to have to put you in a bathtub. OK. So there's a bathtub in one of the rooms and I take my clothing off. And there she comes in and I says, ma soeur! Sister! She says, I've seen them all.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was sulfur. She plugged me up with sulfur and brushed everything off with the brush. I almost screamed blood murder, it was hurting. And then she put some sulfur on and bandages, and got me into bed. I slept like an angel. And the next morning the skin was clear. That's what sulfur does.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the doctor came by, looked at it, and says OK, stay in bed here three days, and let it completely heal. And then we'll talk about what to do and what not to do. Anyhow, short story, I stayed 10 weeks in the hospital as an interpreter. I walked with the mother superior, the head mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was just terrific. I learned about medicine and he took me to surgery. I saw the cutting of limbs and all this, and he was a hell of a teacher. And I had a good time. And more and more came, because they got injured at work. Jackie came with the scabs. So after 10 weeks, unfortunately somebody stole some bread. And that's not the thing to do. So I was made the scapegoat, because I was supposed to tell them this is a good hospital. You don't steal here. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent me back to the camp. I stayed two weeks and I got it again. Back to the hospital, not as an interpreter, just as a sick person.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a week later a whole truckload of SS came and said, everybody out. And the sister came to me, Soeur St. Benoir, and says, Louis, Lutz, stay here. Stay here. I'll hide you under my [NON-ENGLISH], under my robes. Don't go. I said, I can't do that. I've got to go with my friends. We went back to the camp, all of us, even the ones who were operated on the evening before. We didn't even go into the camp. The train was already waiting there. And it's ironic how the Germans react and do not react. We come to the train and the blond SS man is there. He introduces me to the [GERMAN] which were the accompanying gendarmes, the Germans gendarmes. And he says, this little guy is OK. Treat him well. And then he walks away. And as he walks away, and he's about one train away-- you know, one compartment away-- he looks at me, [GERMAN] Dirty Jew. And I got one hit in the cheek. I mean, he almost killed me. That's the way they reacted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So into the train we went. Jackie and I went into one compartment. And out of Boulogne-sur-Mer, back to Brussels. We arrive at about 5:30 in the evening, between 5:00 and 5:30, in the gare du nord again, the north station. And we don't know what's going on. And at this point, you're riding a regular train? Still a regular train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Regular Yeah, train with doors opening. Yeah. No cattle cars yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we don't know what's happening. We don't know where we're going. I mean, Auschwitz was an unknown name. We didn't know. Neither did we know, the time we were in Boulogne-sur-Mer, that by this time, they had already picked up people in Brussels, thousands of people, and send them to Poland. The [NON-ENGLISH]. We didn't know about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as we stand to wait for what, I had no idea, a cattle car comes by, with wagons. And up in the little opening there is a voice saying, [GERMAN] Poland. We're going to Poland. That's the only time we knew we were going to Poland. That was all. So the train gets hooked on and out we go. It's drizzling a little bit. And in the middle of the ride, about nine, ten o'clock in the evening, Jackie says, Louis, look out the window. I said, yeah. What do you see? I see shadows. What else? Men are jumping out of the train. He says, yup. That's what we're going to do. I says, you're nuts. Well, where are we going to go? We have no hair. Dirty clothing. Where are we going to go? He says, we'll go back to Brussels. I said, we have no money. Leave it to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are right close to the border. The border of Belgium and Germany at that time was the Meuse. It's called the [INAUDIBLE] channel. And shortly before he says, Jackie, Louis, now is the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we open the window. He jumped out. A man in between jumped out. I jump out and as I stand on my feet on the gravel while the train is moving, there is a field gendarme, a German field gendarme, looking right into my face.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, it is always said that the human being thinks and God does the rest. I stood at attention. In a magnificent German, I said to-- military German-- I have to go to the front. It's an order from the commandant. And he says OK, jahwol. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, no idea why-- it was dark. He couldn't really recognize me. He didn't know if I was an army man or not. Anyhow, he went on and I went down. And by the ramps, when the train goes this way, you have always grass going down in sand. And there was barbed wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I hear my name calling, Lutz. I said, where? And there's Jackie under the wire. He mistook his steps and got caught. So I took the wire up, he crawled out. And while we crawled out, we wanted to go down, and we look up where the train passed by. And we can see German helmets. And I said, Jackie, now. And we ran down the hill.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the moment we came down, tumbled down, we came down on the ground, we ran. And the shots were whistling. How we never-- it was dark. They couldn't really shoot accurately. We came into a farmhouse. We went up into the where all the bales of hay where. And we hid underneath the bales. Let me tell you. My figuring was, because you didn't hear any shots anymore, the train continued. My figuring was, for two hours, I trembled under this straw. Fear, excitement, I don't know. I couldn't stop it. And then finally I fell asleep. The next morning at about, you know, seven o'clock or something like that, the rooster was crowing, we went down to the owner of the farmhouse and we told him who we are. And he says, oh yeah, okay. He gave us some money. He gave us some food. And we walked to the next train station. We went to Liege, which is a beautiful city. Had something to eat there. Continued with the train from the Liege via Bruge, and went into Brussels. And at the train station, we each walked separately. Because you gendarmes, you had field gendarmes. Later on I found out why so much military at the train station, the gare du nord, was because that night 238 fellas jumped the train. And all went back. All but one. One who got shot. The rest of them all went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we come into Brussels. We take the street car, go to where he used to live. And his fiancee, or his girlfriend opened the door. And we went in. So we talked, and talked, and talked. And she talked to us. And she said, you know you can't stay here. It's possible. How many weeks had you been out of Brussels? It had been quite a while from the time that you left--

speaker: interviewee
text: 27Th of June. This is like, November 1. And she told us that everybody got picked up, people are hiding, and so on. And we have no papers. We have no money. We look miserable. I had no soles on my boots. I walked on my socks or whatever it was. This is winter time again. Brussels gets cold, not snowy, but cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, well, listen, hey. In the morning, we hugged. I said, take care Jackie. I'll see you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew Werner's address, so I walked. And I had enough money to take the streetcar. I walked up to Werner's house. And I rang the bell. And out of the window came, he says, Lutz! I says, yeah. Open the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went up. I took a bath. He didn't have any showers. And I cleaned. She fixed some breakfast and we ate, we talked. Now what to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, his mother was a German, not a Jew. Just his father. It was a mixed marriage. And I said, well, not much we can do. I said, you can't go because they're going to pick you up on account of me. And from that day on, till April '43 I spent in freedom. What we call freedom. No money. No paper. I slept in the gutters, in pee houses, in toilets.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't know where to go. I stole food. I scrounged. I went into garbage cans. You think the homeless are runners? We were forerunners. Cold, freezing. It was unbelievably impossible. Who were you with at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: All alone. Nobody opened the door. Everybody was afraid. Your friends were gone. You didn't know anybody. I got picked up by two hookers who knew me. I slept three or four days with them. One even was a colored gal from the Belgian Congo. So I was treated by them. In April 19th, the 19th I went to the basement, 23 we left. On the 10th of April, 9th of April, something like that, 1943. I just turned 17. Just turned 17. I walk across the street, close to the gare du nord again. And there are my two Flemish SS men. The same ones. Now they don't ask any questions anymore. They just pick me up, put me in a street car with them, back to the Gestapo headquarters. There is the stupid Lieutenant Frank again. And he's happy this time. He is gleefully happy. He says, God am I glad. We got you. I've got five more to go. We picked everybody up again. They had all the 238 recaptured by the time we went to Auschwitz. None of them escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed two days in the Gestapo headquarters. One day they did a boxing match with me. The other day they left me in peace.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it is interesting the mentality, because when they took me the first evening, down in the basement, there was already a gentleman there. Don't know who he was. He was crying. And I looked at the guard and I said, I'm hungry. He says, well, all I have is bread and jam. I said, terrific. I ate the whole jar of jam with the whole piece of bread he gave me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to the man, do you use this rug? He says, no, I can't sleep, my wife, my family. I said, good luck, Charlie. I roll myself into the carpet. I slept like a log. This is what the street does to you. The immunity which comes with this kind of life. So two days later we are being transported to Mechelen or Malines, which is the assemblement camp from Belgium going out to Poland. And already when the truck came in, and I jumped from the truck, the windows opened. And all the buddies were there. David was there. Everybody was there already. I greeted them. And in the afternoon another truck came and there was Jackie. Just unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the 19th of April, 1943, they formed a transport. We all got into a cattle wagon this time, and via Poland. This cattle cars usually holds about 40 to 60 horses, or approximately 120 people. We were 167 people in there, from babies, to old, to young, to women, to men. Closed. Two buckets in each corner to go to your duties. Between the 18th of April and 23rd of April when we arrived in Auschwitz in the morning, the door never opened. We had no food. We had no water. We had nothing. And at one time they hoisted me up to the opening and said Louis, we try again. I said, let's see. And the moment I put my head out, there were shots ringing. The gendarmes were right next door. So no escape this time. We arrive at six o'clock in the morning on a ramp in Auschwitz, Auschwitz I, which is the main camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the doors clink open and suddenly you hear hollering, yelling, Germans, dogs. The only time I saw dogs, Shepherds. Inmate clothing people, military, SS people. Out! Out! Out! Out! Out!

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you where?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea. No idea. Out! This is foggy. And there's a huge, big chief SS man. And I can see the insignia of it's a doctor. And then we have to parade by him. He does his famous left and right, and left and right. And I'm looking at what he's doing and I'm seeing the babies, the old people going to one corner. I'm seeing the strong ones going over there. I said, oh, oh, oh. I got wise. I went to the strong ones. There was a tall man. I hid behind him. So nobody saw me. It turned out we were a 145 fellas in the corner to go to work, or whatever it was. I didn't know at that time. We formed also five already. We were counted. The other ones were all marching. As it stands in documents, they were all marching into the gas, right there and then. Nobody stayed alive out of these people. That was it. They caught some women out of the transport. And in two trucks we went to Monowitz, which is Auschwitz III. And we arrived in a camp. It was about eight o'clock, 8:30 in the morning. We stood on one place, that's there. And about half an hour later we understand already this is eating, because we came from a camp. I mean, some of us knew what a camp was like. We ate everything we had yet in sight. We smoked every cigarette we had in sight. And comes inmate number one, Ludwig Wehr. Was a German criminal, professional criminal. And he was the chief of the camp, or the [GERMAN]. Then he greets us in German of course. And he says, gentlemen, you are in Auschwitz III. This is not a sanitarium. This is not home away from home. This is a labor camp. Now, there will be no calling sir, mister. You have no more name. You were assigned a number. You will answer and talk when you are talked to by that number. Anybody you call sir will be punished. You call everybody by the number. You will be assigned an inmate suit like mine, maybe not tailor made, but mine. You will have a cap. You will have no hair. Escaping, gentlemen, is out of the question because the barbed wire is 20,000 volts. You go three meters close to the barbed wire it will suck you in. It'll burn you within 10 seconds. There's a perimeter in between. And there's another barbed wire. So forget it. You will be assigned a barrack, two to the bunk, which is about one meter in width and about three meter in length, which is nine feet. You will be assigned to commando or working unit. You will be clean at all times. And if you live, that's terrific. And if you don't live, well that's just too bad. This is what you call the greeting. Hey, that's greeting.

speaker: interviewer
text: We go first being deloused. We go to the showers. We have to take all our clothing off. Only could we keep our leather shoes, which mine, forget the whole thing. And the belt, and I didn't even have the belt. We enter the shower rooms and somebody looks at me and says, Lutz? I said, Bully.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two brothers in this camp with the name of Schott. Bully Schott One and Bully Schott Two. They were boxers who knew my cousins and who knew me when I was a boy. And he said to me, [GERMAN]. Come later on and I'll talk to you. So we had already connection.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to the showers. You were with Jackie and David at this point also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. They were with me. We went to the showers. We got deloused with the disinfectant. We went in front of the barracks. We got the pants. We got a jacket. And in the summertime you got winter clothing. And in wintertime you got summer clothing. Hey, that's normal. You got a pair of-- I had a very little tiny foot, so I got a pair of leather shoes from some other person who passed away, or whatever. I got a leather belt. All the papers I had were destroyed. Nothing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we were supposed to be tattooed, you see? And I had the scabs. They couldn't tattoo me. So they had to call an SS man, the blockfuhrer, the chief of four blocks. He says, [GERMAN]. He has to be tattooed. [GERMAN]. Tattoo him underneath. OK. So I got my number underneath.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to all of us, [GERMAN]. Do well to remember the number. 117657. Good. Then we got some soup to eat. And it was the only time in all my camp time, we had oatmeal. Oatmeal soup. [GERMAN] And everybody, aside of Jackie, David, Yannick, and two other fellows, they couldn't eat. They were so disturbed by the whole view, by the whole sight, and everything. I ate five quarts of this dumb stuff. It was so sweet. It was so good. I couldn't even move anymore afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
